EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as late afternoon and early evening minor flooding along the Coochs River in New Castle County on the 29th. The rain began around sunrise on the 29th and fell heavy at times from the late morning into the middle of the afternoon of the 29th. The rain ended during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 510 p.m. EST through 750 p.m. EST on the 29th. It crested at 11.03 feet at 645 p.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.37 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 1.32 inches in Newark, 1.25 inches in Prices Corner and 1.20 inches in Odessa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced heavy rain and some wind damage in New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in New Castle County north of the Chesapeake and Delaware Canal between Glasgow and Saint Georges. Several hundred Delmarva Power and Light customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems combined with a high pressure system passing across nearby Canada to bring a wintry mix of precipitation to northern New Castle County on the 14th. Snowfall averaged less than one inch with a brief period of freezing rain toward the Pennsylvania border before precipitation changed to plain rain.   The combination caused some slippery traveling conditions.  Snow started by the middle of the afternoon on the 14th and transitioned over to rain south of the Chesapeake and Delaware Canal. The snow briefly changed to sleet and freezing rain and then just plain rain by late afternoon. Representative snowfall (all in New Castle County) included 0.9 inches in Claymont, 0.7 inches in Hockessin and 0.5 inches at the New Castle County Airport.  |  |The wintry mix was caused by a series of  low pressure systems, one emerging from the central Rockies and the second forming in the western Gulf of Mexico on the evening of the 13th. They reached Missouri and Mississippi respectively on the morning of the 14th. Meanwhile, a high pressure system with a fresh supply of cold air reached southern Quebec Province, Canada.  While the southern low pressure system weakened, the northern one reached the central Ohio Valley at 4 p.m. EST on the 14th as the center of the high pressure system started to cross the Saint Lawrence River Valley and slowly eased east during the evening and overnight. A new low pressure system was starting to form on the warm frontal boundary in North Carolina. This latter low pressure system started to intensify as the Ohio Valley low pressure system started to weaken. At 10 p.m. EST on the 14th, the former low pressure system reached the lower Chesapeake Bay. This low became the main low pressure system and passed just east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 1 a.m. EST on the 15th and eastern Long Island at 7 a.m. EST on the 15th. The sequence of secondary formation helped bring in enough warm air at the surface to negate most of the wintry weather in Delaware.

Strong south winds affected Sussex County in Delaware during the afternoon of the 29th as an intensifying low pressure system passed across central Delaware and the county was in the warm sector for this event. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and combined with the rain, knocked down weak tree limbs and wires. Peak wind gusts included 48 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 46 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County) and at the Indian River Inlet (Sussex County) and 43 mph in Lewes and Georgetown (Sussex County).

Strong south winds affected Sussex County in Delaware during the afternoon of the 29th as an intensifying low pressure system passed across central Delaware and the county was in the warm sector for this event. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and combined with the rain, knocked down weak tree limbs and wires. Peak wind gusts included 48 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 46 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County) and at the Indian River Inlet (Sussex County) and 43 mph in Lewes and Georgetown (Sussex County).

A southeastern United States low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce 2 to 4 inches of snow across New Castle County and less than an inch of snow across the rest of Delaware during the daytime hours on the 10th. The snow in New Castle County caused school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings.  Wilmington, Newark and New Castle County government offices were closed. State offices were also closed in New Castle County. About ten weather related accidents were reported by state police along with four disabled vehicles.  Precipitation started across the state around 7 a.m. EST as snow in New Castle County and rain elsewhere. The snow fell at its heaviest before 10 a.m. EST.  As the low pressure system moved east, the rain changed to snow in Kent County during the late morning and in Sussex County around Noon EST on the 10th. The snow ended between 2 p.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST that afternoon.||Representative snowfall included 3.4 inches in Newark (New Castle County), 3.1 inches in Talleyville (New Castle County), 2.9 inches at the New Castle County Airport and Hockessin (New Castle County), 2.3 inches in Newport (New Castle County), 2.1 inches in Odessa (New Castle County) and 0.4 inches in Dover (Kent County).||The low pressure system responsible for the snow formed along a stationary front in the southeastern United States overnight on the 9th. At 7 a.m. EST on the 10th, it was located over western North Carolina. It proceeded to move east and reached the North Carolina coastal waters at 7 p.m. EST that evening and moved farther east offshore overnight.

Heavy rain associated with a low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico caused poor drainage as well as moderate flooding along the Christina River on the 27th.  The rain also hampered getaway plans for travelers for the Thanksgiving holiday weekend. Rain began on the morning of the 26th and started falling heavy at times during the late evening of the 26th. The rain continued to fall heavy at times overnight.  Lighter rain then continued into the afternoon of the 27th before ending. Event precipitation totals average 2.5 to 3.0 inches in New Castle County and 1.0 to 2.5 inches in Kent and Sussex Counties.  The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that emerged from the Gulf of Mexico on the 26th. The low moved northeast and reached the Carolinas during the evening of the 26th, Maryland early on the 27th and passed near New York City at 7 a.m. EST on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Christina River at Coochs Bridge had moderate flooding and was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 505 a.m. EST through 936 a.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 11.54 feet at 715 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 3.12 inches in Newport, 3.01 inches in Hockessin, 3.00 inches in Prices Corner, 2.56 inches in Newark and 2.51 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meteotsunami believed to have been caused by the passing of a weak end derecho moving off the Delaware coast occurred around Noon EDT on the 13th. The weak end derecho moved off the Delaware coast at about 11 a.m. EDT on the morning of the 13th. The surface pressure fluctuation caused by this phenomenon was believed to have triggered it. The tsunami measured approximately five inches at Lewes.  No damage or injuries were reported along the Delaware Coast.  ||Other meteotsunamis occurred along the east coast in Boothbay Harbor, Maine in 2008 and in Daytona Beach, Florida in 1992.

An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. The most severe storms affected New Castle County.  Delmarva Power and Light reported about 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state and power was not fully restored until the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees along Brackenville Road in Mill Creek Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. The most severe storms affected New Castle County.  Delmarva Power and Light reported about 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state and power was not fully restored until the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of funnel clouds were spotted as a thunderstorm crossed northern New Castle County from Newark to Bear. None of them touched the ground or caused any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. The most severe storms affected New Castle County.  Delmarva Power and Light reported about 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state and power was not fully restored until the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires in and around Christina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved through Delaware and although amounts varied across the state, in general 1 to 3 inches of rain was reported. A daily rainfall record was recorded at the Georgetown Airport in Sussex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy rain fell across central and eastern portions of Sussex County. A record daily maximum rainfall of 2.32 inches was recorded at the Georgetown Airport on the 10th. Additional rainfall totals include 3.03 inches in Milton, 1.28 inches in Longneck and 0.70 inches in Seaford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved through Delaware and although amounts varied across the state, in general 1 to 3 inches of rain was reported. A daily rainfall record was recorded at the Georgetown Airport in Sussex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across central and northern New Castle County, which caused the Christina River to overflow its banks. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge had moderate flooding and was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 614 p.m. EDT through 1016 p.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 11.54 feet at 815 p.m. EDT. Additional rainfall amounts include 1.70 inches in Prices Corner, 1.62 inches in Newark, 1.56 inches in Newport, 1.44 inches in Clayton and 1.06 inches in Middletown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. The most severe storms affected New Castle County.  Delmarva Power and Light reported about 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state and power was not fully restored until the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in Hockessin (Mill Creek Hundred).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in New Castle County that started during the late afternoon on the 28th and continued  overnight.  Major flooding occurred along the Christina River and moderate flooding occurred along the White Clay Creek. An average of 1.5 to 3.0 inches of rain fell across the northern tier of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding across the northern tier of New Castle County from Newark east through New Castle and northward to the Pennsylvania border. In Newark, Paper Mill Road was flooded from a tributary of the White Clay Creek. At least one vehicle was stuck in flood waters along the White Clay Creek itself.  Several vehicles were stuck in high water along South Chapel Street. Near Newark, flooding occurred on Delaware State Routes 2 and 7 and water rescues were performed. The Persimmon Creek flooded over a roadway and the Christina River flooded parkland. Flooding also occurred along Delaware State Route 1 and Interstate 95 in Christina Hundred. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge had major flooding and was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 653 p.m. EDT on the 28th through 251 a.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 12.89 feet at 1115 p.m. EDT on the 28th. The White Clay Creek at Delaware Park had moderate flooding and was above its 13 foot flood stage from 649 p.m. EDT on the 28th through 154 a.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 15.25 feet at 945 p.m. EDT on the 28th.  Event precipitation totals included 3.55 inches in Newark, 2.49 inches in Newport, 2.37 inches in Wilmington and 2.13 inches in Hockessin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with a hot and humid air mass to produce strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the second half of the afternoon and the evening on the 24th. The strongest storms affected New Castle County during the evening. Approximately 2,500 Delmarva Power and Light customers lost power.  Power was restored by the 25th. There were also some reports of hail with the thunderstorms as the freezing level aloft was relatively low in height above the ground. Most of the hail was small (less than half an inch).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Wilmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first run of hot weather that combined high temperatures in the 90s and more oppressive humidity levels affected Delaware on July 6th and 7th. High temperatures both days reached into the lower 90s and combined with dew points of around 70 degrees produced afternoon heat index values of near 100 degrees.  High temperatures were about the same both days, but the 7th was a slightly more humid day. Morning low temperatures were mainly in the lower to mid 70s.  The passage of a weak trof dropped temperatures slightly on the 8th.||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers.  Highest temperatures included 94 degrees in Wilmington (New Castle County) and Prime Hook (Sussex County), 93 degrees in Newark (New Castle County), Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County) and Smyrna (Kent County), 92 degrees at the New Castle County Airport, Georgetown (Sussex County) and 91 degrees in Dover (Kent County).

A low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce showers and thunderstorms with very heavy downpours and led to flooding and flash flooding in Delaware. The heaviest rain fell during the first half of the day in Sussex County, into the afternoon in Kent County and into the evening in New Castle County. About 40 roadways were flooded and closed in Kent and Sussex Counties. While nearly the whole state received an inch of rain, sections of Kent and Sussex Counties received 4 to 8 inches and these locations had the worst flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain led to flash flooding from Selbyville east in southern Sussex County. A few roads were closed and sections of Lighthouse and Sand Cove Roads collapsed.  Event precipitation totals included 5.50 inches in Selbyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce showers and thunderstorms with very heavy downpours and led to flooding and flash flooding in Delaware. The heaviest rain fell during the first half of the day in Sussex County, into the afternoon in Kent County and into the evening in New Castle County. About 40 roadways were flooded and closed in Kent and Sussex Counties. While nearly the whole state received an inch of rain, sections of Kent and Sussex Counties received 4 to 8 inches and these locations had the worst flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain caused flooding along the Saint Jones River twice on the 12th and 13th. The Saint Jones River in Dover was above its 6 foot flood stage from 542 p.m. EDT through 730 p.m. EDT on the 12th and again from 1254 a.m. EDT through 637 p.m. EDT on the 13th. Its highest crest was 6.80 feet at 715 a.m. EDT on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce showers and thunderstorms with very heavy downpours and led to flooding and flash flooding in Delaware. The heaviest rain fell during the first half of the day in Sussex County, into the afternoon in Kent County and into the evening in New Castle County. About 40 roadways were flooded and closed in Kent and Sussex Counties. While nearly the whole state received an inch of rain, sections of Kent and Sussex Counties received 4 to 8 inches and these locations had the worst flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of roadways and small streams and eventually flooding along the Saint Jones River in central Kent County. In Dover, several vehicles became stranded in flood waters as numerous roadways flooded. Several stranded motorists had to abandon their vehicles. The water was also several feet deep at the Kent General Hospital in Dover. The Little Creek in Port Mahon flooded and shut down Main Street and Delaware State Route 9. Numerous basements were flooded. Event precipitation totals included 5.46 inches in Dover, 3.49 inches in Sandtown, 3.45 inches at the Dover Air Force Base and 2.46 inches in Smyrna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected Delaware from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only around 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 110 degrees. There was one heat related death reported in New Castle (New Castle County). A 55-year-old man was found outdoors and unresponsive. | |To combat the heat, many municipalities opened or extended hours of cooling centers.  Highest temperatures included 96 degrees in Georgetown, Redden and Bethany Beach in Sussex County, 95 degrees in Dover (Kent County) and the New Castle County Airport and 92 degrees in Blackbird (New Castle County).  A stalling front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd in the northern part of the state and the 23rd in the southern part of the state.

High water was reported on Hardscrabble Road (Delaware State Route 20), Shortly Road, Kendall Street and Laurel Road (Delaware State Route 24). Event precipitation totals included 4.20 inches in Stockley and 3.70 inches in Georgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce showers and thunderstorms with very heavy downpours and led to flooding and flash flooding in Delaware. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding from Millville and Oceanview east to Bethany Beach. Water rescues were performed, but no serious injuries were reported. In Bethany Beach, Hollywood Street and Pennsylvania Avenuie were flooded as people resorted to kayaking to move around. Event precipitation totals included 6.62 inches in Bethany Beach.

A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused flash flooding in the northern half of Delaware. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in northeastern New Castle County and around 6 inches along the New Castle County and Kent County border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused urban, small stream and poor drainage flash flooding in northeastern New Castle County from around Bear northeast through Claymont.  Poor drainage flooding occurred on U.S. Route 13 north of Rogers Road in Wilmington. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 2 to 4 inches in this area. Actual rainfall amounts included 2.32 inches in Wilmington and 1.72 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea moving up the Eastern Seaboard, from the Southeast States to the Mid Atlantic and Northeast Coasts, led to waves of heavy rain and thunderstorms from the early morning hours through the late evening on the 7th.  The initial burst of heavy precipitation arrived during the early morning on the 7th as moisture surged into the Mid Atlantic Region ahead of Tropical Storm Andrea, with the center of its low pressure located near the coast of Georgia.  Through the course of the day, the cyclone became post-tropical as it accelerated northeastward along the East Coast of the United States, delivering another round of heavy rain with some strong thunderstorms to the Mid Atlantic States during the late afternoon and evening of the 7th.||Doppler radar storm total estimates were mostly in the 2 to 4 inch range across Delaware, with some estimates as high as 6 inches through the central part of the state.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and flash flooding, which caused multiple road closures, especially in central Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flash flooding along some roadways and along smaller rivers in Kent County. Road closures were reported during the evening of the 7th. Of note, Saulsbury Road in Dover was closed due to flooding. In addition, the Saint Jones River at Dover was above its 6 foot flood stage from 511 a.m. EDT through 1022 p.m. EDT on the 8th, and it crested at 6.62 feet at 1215 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 5.19 inches in Smyrna, 4.83 inches in Dover, and 3.70 inches in Felton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a tornado and wind damage in western New Castle County during the afternoon of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado was confirmed near Newark at 447 p.m. EDT. The damage was focused from Ardmore Drive to Argyle Road, south of DE Route 4. Within this area, 10 to 20 homes sustained damage with several homes suffering significant damage due to large trees being blown into them. On Natalie Lane, a very large tree completely fell over and landed onto two homes with a car having significant damage. Other homes suffered siding and shingle damage. One house on Argyle Road had a tree limb through a side wall. On Havertown Road, several trees were uprooted, with some lying west and northwest, which appeared to be the western edge of the circulation. Several residents reported hearing a whooshing sound that lasted for about 10 to 15 seconds, along with some tree debris swirling into the air. Based on damage reports, the tornado briefly lifted with the main touchdown located from Ardmore Drive to Argyle Road. ||Additional damage was reported to the south of the tornado path, including siding and tree damage. One she was reportedly blown onto its side along McCormick Boulevard. The overall path length was nearly one mile. The maximum path width was 150 yards and the estimated maximum wind was 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning low end derecho moved through the region on the 13th which caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across Delaware. The derecho moved off the New Jersey coast by late morning leaving a boundary across parts of the region. Later in the day, a strong low pressure system brought a cold front through the region, which sparked off more strong to severe thunderstorms across the area. ||In addition to wind, heavy rain was reported with these storms. Rainfall amounts were around 1 to 1.50 inches, with the heaviest amounts across Kent County. Rainfall amounts included 1.45 inches in Smyrna (Kent County), 1.26 inches in Newark (New Castle County), 1.16 inches in Dover (Kent County), 1.10 inches in Newport (New Castle County) 0.79 inches in Selbyville (Sussex County) and 0.67 inches in Delmar (Sussex County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms knocked down numerous trees and wires with scattered power outages in New Castle County. One downed tree damaged a home in New Castle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea moving up the Eastern Seaboard, from the Southeast States to the Mid Atlantic and Northeast Coasts, led to waves of heavy rain and thunderstorms from the early morning hours through the late evening on the 7th.  The initial burst of heavy precipitation arrived during the early morning on the 7th as moisture surged into the Mid Atlantic Region ahead of Tropical Storm Andrea, with the center of its low pressure located near the coast of Georgia.  Through the course of the day, the cyclone became post-tropical as it accelerated northeastward along the East Coast of the United States, delivering another round of heavy rain with some strong thunderstorms to the Mid Atlantic States during the late afternoon and evening of the 7th.||Doppler radar storm total estimates were mostly in the 2 to 4 inch range across Delaware, with some estimates as high as 6 inches through the central part of the state.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and flash flooding, which caused multiple road closures, especially in central Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flash flooding along several gaged rivers and creeks in New Castle County. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge reported moderate flooding and was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 306 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 1250 a.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 12.44 feet at 830 p.m. EDT on the 7th. The White Clay Creek at Delaware Park was above its 13 foot flood stage from 405 p.m. EDT through 1102 p.m. EDT on the 7th, and it crested at 13.99 feet at 845 p.m. EDT. The Red Clay Creek at Wooddale was above its 7 foot flood stage from 745 p.m. EDT through 922 p.m. EDT on the 7th, and it crested at 7.01 feet at 815 p.m. EDT. The Red Clay Creek at Stanton was above its 15 foot flood stage from 632 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 1219 a.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 16.02 feet at 830 p.m. EDT on the 7th. Event precipitation totals included 4.52 inches in Newark, 4.48 inches in Hockessin, 4.18 inches in Newport, 4.10 inches in Wilmington, 4.09 inches in Glasgow, and 4.01 inches in Middletown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a tornado and wind damage in western New Castle County during the afternoon of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Preceding the tornado touch down, the same parent thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Glasgow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper air disturbance coupled with a weak surface trof and hot and humid weather developed a line of strong to isolated severe thunderstorms that passed across northern New Castle County during the early evening of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees across northern New Castle County. About 500 Delmarva Power homes and businesses lost power.

About 40 roadways were flooded and closed in Kent and Sussex Counties. While nearly the whole state received an inch of rain, sections of Kent and Sussex Counties received 4 to 8 inches and these locations had the worst flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain led to flash flooding in the Long Neck area of Indian River Hundred. Flood waters reached a foot deep on roadways. Event precipitation totals included 8.38 inches in Long Neck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowing cold front coupled with a sea breeze front caused nearly stationary thunderstorms to form in southern Delaware and led to flash flooding in Sussex County. Also the combination, of the full moon and some of the highest astronomical tides of 2013, caused minor tidal flooding to occur along the Delaware shore during the evening high tide cycle on the 21st through the 23rd and again on the 25th. The highest tide (on the evening high tide cycle on the 25th) at Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County) was 6.37 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water. In Upper Delaware Bay and the tidal Delaware River, minor tidal flooding also occurred during the late evening high tide cycles on the 22nd and 23rd. At Reedy Island (New Castle County), the highest tide reached 7.52 feet above mean lower low water on the 22nd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary thunderstorms dropped heavy rain and caused flash flooding in south central Delaware mainly to the southwest of Millsboro. There was widespread ponding of water on roadways, construction equipment was seen floating in the flood waters. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded four inches southwest of Millsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce showers and thunderstorms with very heavy downpours and led to flooding and flash flooding in Delaware. The heaviest rain fell during the first half of the day in Sussex County, into the afternoon in Kent County and into the evening in New Castle County. About 40 roadways were flooded and closed in Kent and Sussex Counties. While nearly the whole state received an inch of rain, sections of Kent and Sussex Counties received 4 to 8 inches and these locations had the worst flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of roadways and small streams in and around Frankford. Event precipitation totals included 7.05 inches in Frankford.

About 40 roadways were flooded and closed in Kent and Sussex Counties. While nearly the whole state received an inch of rain, sections of Kent and Sussex Counties received 4 to 8 inches and these locations had the worst flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of roadways and small streams from Dagsboro east along the southern shore of the Indian River Bay.  High water was reported on Vines Creek Road, Warren Wingate Lane, Holts Landing Road and Waltersbluff Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain embedded within a general area of rain and wet antecedent conditions led to poor drainage flooding and flash flooding of creeks and rivers in Delaware. The heaviest rain fell during the afternoon and event precipitation totals averaged one to three inches across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along  the Christina River. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 314 p.m. EDT through 609 p.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 10.94 feet at 415 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with a hot and humid air mass to produce strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the second half of the afternoon and the evening on the 24th. The strongest storms affected New Castle County during the evening. Approximately 2,500 Delmarva Power and Light customers lost power.  Power was restored by the 25th. There were also some reports of hail with the thunderstorms as the freezing level aloft was relatively low in height above the ground. Most of the hail was small (less than half an inch).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several  trees and wires in northeastern New Castle County from Talleyville to Claymont. Shipley Road was closed because of downed trees overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and lee side trof helped trigger strong to locally severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 17th with New Castle County affected the most.  The thunderstorms also produced small hail. About 11,000 Delmarva Power and Light customers lost power. As off 11 p.m. EDT  more than half were still without power. The outages were concentrated in and around Wilmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and large tree limbs in Winterthur in Christina Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof helped develop thunderstorms with heavy rain that fell across the headwaters of the Christina River Basin during the late afternoon and early evening of the 17th. Rainfall totals averaged 1 to 2 inches and caused moderate flooding along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Christina River at Coochs Bridge had moderate flooding and was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 716 p.m. EDT through 1101 p.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 11.80 feet at 715 p.m. EDT.  Flooding was reported on Delaware State Route 72 near Interstate 95 in Newark. Flooding was also reported along U.S. Route 40 in Bear.  Event precipitation totals included 1.81 inches in Bear, 1.73 inches in Newark, 1.21 inches in Middletown and 1.10 inches in Hockessin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in Port Penn. Delaware State Route 9 was closed and several cars had to be abandoned. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding in northern New Castle County during the afternoon of the 30th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded two inches across the width of the county from Glasgow to New Castle. Extremely wet antecedent conditions made it easier for flash flooding to occur. ||It was the wettest June on record at the New Castle County Airport. The 13.66 inches of rain that fell thoroughly thrashed the previous June monthly record of 9.90 inches set in 2003. The June monthly rainfall total in Georgetown was 12.24 inches, but was not a new June monthly record. On a statewide average, the June rainfall total of 10.94 inches was the wettest June on record dating back through 1895.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in west central New Castle County from Glasgow north to near Newark. Event precipitation totals included 2.15 inches in Glasgow and 1.69 inches in Newark.

No injuries were reported.  Event precipitation totals included 1.75 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning low end derecho moved through the region on the 13th which caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across Delaware. The derecho moved off the New Jersey coast by late morning leaving a boundary across parts of the region. Later in the day, a strong low pressure system brought a cold front through the region, which sparked off more strong to severe thunderstorms across the area. ||In addition to wind, heavy rain was reported with these storms. Rainfall amounts were around 1 to 1.50 inches, with the heaviest amounts across Kent County. Rainfall amounts included 1.45 inches in Smyrna (Kent County), 1.26 inches in Newark (New Castle County), 1.16 inches in Dover (Kent County), 1.10 inches in Newport (New Castle County) 0.79 inches in Selbyville (Sussex County) and 0.67 inches in Delmar (Sussex County).

Some of the downed trees damaged vehicles and homes. One home on South Rockfield Drive had its roof, garage and gutter damaged by a downed tree. Ramsey Road and Thompson Bridge Road were closed because of multiple downed trees. In addition, numerous trees were knocked down along Naamans Road. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved off the North Carolina coast during the evening of the 25th helped drop around one inch of snow across Delaware. The least snow fell in New Castle County while the most fell along coastal Sussex County.  The snow unfortunately coincided nearly perfectly with the Friday evening commute and resulted in numerous, but mostly minor, accidents and very long commute times. The snow began falling around 3 p.m. EST on the 25th. The snow stuck instantly as temperatures were well below freezing. The snow ended between 630 p.m. EST and 8 p.m. EST on the 25th.  Delaware State Police reported about 40 traffic accidents, the least in New Castle County. Five of the accidents resulted in injuries and there were also four disabled vehicles.||Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County), 1.5 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County) 1.4 inches in Seaford (Sussex County), 1.2 inches in Magnolia (Kent County), 1.0 inch in Viola (Kent County), Harrington (Kent County) and Ellendale (Sussex County), 0.9 inches in Delmar and Bridgeville in Sussex County, 0.8 inches in Newark (New Castle County) and Dover (Kent County) and 0.6 inches at the New Castle County Airport.|  |The low pressure system moved from the southern Rockies on the morning of the 24th into the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 25th and reached Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 25th. It then proceeded quickly offshore to the northeast. The fast movement limited the amount of snow that fell.

A wintry mix of precipitation affected Delaware during the early morning of the 28th before changing to all rain later that morning. The effects of the wintry mix increased farther north in the state.  All places away from the immediate coast had at least some freezing rain. While snowfall amounts were less than an inch, any freezing rain froze quickly as this precipitation followed several days of unseasonably cold weather. Untreated roadways and walkways were slippery.   ||Steady precipitation started across the state between 6 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST on the 28th as snow and some sleet. Precipitation changed to freezing rain between 730 a.m. EST and 830 a.m. EST from south to north and to plain rain from south to north between 9 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST.  Periods of rain then fell into early afternoon.  ||Snowfall amounts included 0.3 inches in Newark (New Castle County). 0.2 inch at Bethany Beach and Milton (Sussex County) and 0.1 of an inch at Magnolia (Kent County) and Seaford (Sussex County).  Ice accumulations averaged a couple hundredths of an inch throughout the state.  The wintry weather was caused by a low pressure system and its associated warm front that emerged from the central Rockies on the morning of the 27th, reached Iowa on the evening of the 27th,  southeast Wisconsin on the morning of the 28th and southwest Ontario province on the evening of the 28th. Precipitation ended as its associated warm front neared Delaware and a secondary low pressure system formed east of New Jersey.

A pair of quick moving low pressure systems dropped around an inch of snow across Delaware during the evening and overnight on the 2nd. While the snowfall was light,  the subfreezing temperatures helped it accumulate efficiently and caused untreated roadways to quickly become treacherous. Delaware State Police responded to around fifteen accidents, three with injuries. In Sussex County, both the Nassau and Love Creek Bridges were very slippery. In Kent County, many untreated roadways had black ice on them. In New Castle County, accidents forced lane closures on Interstates 495 over the Christina River Bridge and on Interstate 95.  In addition, there were four disabled vehicles. Snow began falling across the state between 7 p.m. EST and 8 p.m. EST on the 2nd. The snow ended between 1030 p.m. EST and Midnight EST that evening in Kent and Sussex Counties and between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 3rd in New Castle County. Representative snowfall included 1.1 inches in Seaford (Sussex County), 1.0 inch in Newark (New Castle County), 0.7 inches in Milton (Sussex County) and the New Castle County Airport and 0.5 inches in Harrington (Kent County). The snow was caused by double barrel low pressure systems. First an Alberta Clipper low pressure system that rapidly moved east from near Chicago on the morning of the 2nd,  to Lake Erie on the evening of the 2nd. This low pressure system then slowed and weakened as a southern stream low pressure system moved through the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 2nd and moved through Virginia overnight on the 2nd and intensified as it moved well east of the Middle Atlantic Coast on the 3rd.

A low pressure system that formed along a secondary cold front helped drop two to six inches of snow across the southern half of New Castle County, Kent County and the northern half of Sussex County during the morning of the 1st. The heaviest snowfall was right around Dover. The snow fell at its heaviest during the morning commute and caused over 150 accidents with at least a dozen that caused injuries.||The snow began falling across the state between 530 a.m. EST and 630 a.m. EST on the 1st. It fell at its heaviest between 7 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST and ended between 10 a.m. EST and 1130 a.m. EST that morning. Delaware State Police reported around 60 accidents (5 with injuries) in Kent County, nearly 100 accidents (10 with injuries) in New Castle County  and 4 accidents (1 with injuries) in Sussex County. There were also about a dozen disabled vehicles.  In New Castle County, the Summit Bridge (U.S. Route 301) was closed until it could be salted. In Tybouts, Northbound U.S. Route 13 was closed.  In Kent County, blowing snow complicated roadway plowing along Delaware State Route 1 around Dover.||Representative snowfall included 5.9 inches in Dover (Kent County), 3.2 inches in Magnolia (Kent County), 3.0 inches in Harrington (Kent County) and Blackbird (New Castle County), 2.7 inches in Viola (Kent County), 2.2 inches in Milton (Sussex County), 2.0 inches in Lewes (Sussex County), 1.8 inches in Odessa (New Castle County), 1.4 inches in Glasgow (New Castle County), 1.3 inches in Seaford (Sussex County) and 0.5 inches in Bridgeville (Sussex County). ||The snow was caused by a compact and quick moving low pressure system that formed along the northern edge of a secondary cold front. Strong to high winds occurred across Delaware from around Midnight EST into the middle of the afternoon on the 31st.  Peak wind gusts reached between 45 mph and 65 mph and downed weak trees, tree limbs and power lines and caused scattered outages. Delmarva Power reported about 5,400 of its customers lost power. All of the high winds and most of the highest wind gusts occurred in the south to southwest flow preceding a cold frontal passage during the early morning on the 31st. However, strong wind gusts near 50 mph continued in the westerly flow following the cold frontal passage during the morning and early afternoon on the 31st.  The cold front moved through the state between 5 a.m.EST  and 6 a.m. EST. ||Peak wind gusts included 63 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 61 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 56 mph in Dover (Kent County) and Georgetown (Sussex County) and 47 mph at the New Castle County Airport.||The strong to high winds were caused by a land bombing low pressure system that intensified rapidly as it moved through the Great Lakes on January 30th and into Quebec Province on January 31st.  The central pressure of the low pressure system over Illinois deepened from 992 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 30th to 968 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st when it was in Quebec Province.  The low pressure system continued to deepen and the following morning, the pressure was 944 millibars as it was crossing the Labrador Sea (The lowest analyzed pressure with this system was 940 millibars.).  The rapid deepening of the surface low intensified the southerly flow preceding its cold front and then the pressure gradient (difference) between the low pressure system and a high pressure system entering the Northern Plains continued strong winds into the afternoon of the 31st.  The low pressure system movement away from Delaware caused the strong winds to stop later that day.

While the snowfall was light,  the subfreezing temperatures helped it accumulate efficiently and caused untreated roadways to quickly become treacherous. Delaware State Police responded to around fifteen accidents, three with injuries. In Sussex County, both the Nassau and Love Creek Bridges were very slippery. In Kent County, many untreated roadways had black ice on them. In New Castle County, accidents forced lane closures on Interstates 495 over the Christina River Bridge and on Interstate 95.  In addition, there were four disabled vehicles. Snow began falling across the state between 7 p.m. EST and 8 p.m. EST on the 2nd. The snow ended between 1030 p.m. EST and Midnight EST that evening in Kent and Sussex Counties and between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 3rd in New Castle County. Representative snowfall included 1.1 inches in Seaford (Sussex County), 1.0 inch in Newark (New Castle County), 0.7 inches in Milton (Sussex County) and the New Castle County Airport and 0.5 inches in Harrington (Kent County). The snow was caused by double barrel low pressure systems. First an Alberta Clipper low pressure system that rapidly moved east from near Chicago on the morning of the 2nd,  to Lake Erie on the evening of the 2nd. This low pressure system then slowed and weakened as a southern stream low pressure system moved through the Tennessee Valley on the evening of the 2nd and moved through Virginia overnight on the 2nd and intensified as it moved well east of the Middle Atlantic Coast on the 3rd.

A rapidly intensifying ocean storm that moved south to north over the western Atlantic produced strong gusty west to northwest winds from the afternoon on the 17th into the early morning on the 18th in Delaware. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused isolated power outages. Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Odessa (New Castle County), 54 mph in Newark (New Castle County) and 49 mph in Lewes (Sussex County). ||The northward moving ocean storm intensified from a 1001 millibar low pressure system east of North Carolina on the evening of the 16th, to a 979 millibar low pressure system well east of New Jersey on the morning of the 17th and peaked as a 954 millibar low pressure system over Nova Scotia at 7 p.m. EST on the 17th. From there it slowly weakened as it moved toward Newfoundland overnight. As this low moved farther away from Delaware, the strong winds ceased.

Strong to high winds occurred across Delaware from around Midnight EST into the middle of the afternoon on the 31st.  Peak wind gusts reached between 45 mph and 65 mph and downed weak trees, tree limbs and power lines and caused scattered outages. Delmarva Power reported about 5,400 of its customers lost power. All of the high winds and most of the highest wind gusts occurred in the south to southwest flow preceding a cold frontal passage during the early morning on the 31st. However, strong wind gusts near 50 mph continued in the westerly flow following the cold frontal passage during the morning and early afternoon on the 31st.  The cold front moved through the state between 5 a.m.EST  and 6 a.m. EST. ||Peak wind gusts included 63 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 61 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 56 mph in Dover (Kent County) and Georgetown (Sussex County) and 47 mph at the New Castle County Airport.||The strong to high winds were caused by a land bombing low pressure system that intensified rapidly as it moved through the Great Lakes on January 30th and into Quebec Province on January 31st.  The central pressure of the low pressure system over Illinois deepened from 992 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 30th to 968 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st when it was in Quebec Province.  The low pressure system continued to deepen and the following morning, the pressure was 944 millibars as it was crossing the Labrador Sea (The lowest analyzed pressure with this system was 940 millibars.).  The rapid deepening of the surface low intensified the southerly flow preceding its cold front and then the pressure gradient (difference) between the low pressure system and a high pressure system entering the Northern Plains continued strong winds into the afternoon of the 31st.  The low pressure system movement away from Delaware caused the strong winds to stop later that day.

Dense fog affected travel on both the mornings of the 12th and 13th in Delaware. The most widespread and greatest impact on traveling occurred on the morning of the 13th.  The fog was caused by a relatively moist and stagnant flow over the region on that weekend. An approaching cold front started mixing the air further and ended the perpetuation of morning dense fog.

A low pressure system that formed along a secondary cold front helped drop two to six inches of snow across the southern half of New Castle County, Kent County and the northern half of Sussex County during the morning of the 1st. The heaviest snowfall was right around Dover. The snow fell at its heaviest during the morning commute and caused over 150 accidents with at least a dozen that caused injuries.||The snow began falling across the state between 530 a.m. EST and 630 a.m. EST on the 1st. It fell at its heaviest between 7 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST and ended between 10 a.m. EST and 1130 a.m. EST that morning. Delaware State Police reported around 60 accidents (5 with injuries) in Kent County, nearly 100 accidents (10 with injuries) in New Castle County  and 4 accidents (1 with injuries) in Sussex County. There were also about a dozen disabled vehicles.  In New Castle County, the Summit Bridge (U.S. Route 301) was closed until it could be salted. In Tybouts, Northbound U.S. Route 13 was closed.  In Kent County, blowing snow complicated roadway plowing along Delaware State Route 1 around Dover.||Representative snowfall included 5.9 inches in Dover (Kent County), 3.2 inches in Magnolia (Kent County), 3.0 inches in Harrington (Kent County) and Blackbird (New Castle County), 2.7 inches in Viola (Kent County), 2.2 inches in Milton (Sussex County), 2.0 inches in Lewes (Sussex County), 1.8 inches in Odessa (New Castle County), 1.4 inches in Glasgow (New Castle County), 1.3 inches in Seaford (Sussex County) and 0.5 inches in Bridgeville (Sussex County). ||The snow was caused by a compact and quick moving low pressure system that formed along the northern edge of a secondary cold front.  The low pressure system moved from Lake Erie during the early evening of January 31st eastsoutheast and passed across the northern part of the state around 7 a.m. EST on February 1st and exited just as quickly offshore.

The strong south to southwest winds preceding a cold frontal passage brought an unseasonably mild and moist air mass into Delaware. This caused heavy rain to fall in New Castle County and caused poor drainage flooding and isolated river flooding. Event totals averaged 1.0 to 1.5 inches in New Castle County. Lower amounts fell elsewhere in the state. The rain began during the late afternoon on the 30th and fell at its heaviest during the early morning of the 31st as convective elements moved through the state. The rain ended by 6 a.m. EST on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5  foot flood stage from 543 a.m. EST through 754 a.m. EST on the 31st.  It crested at 10.77 feet at 7 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.58 inches in Hockessin, 1.38 inches in Newark, 1.26 inches at the New Castle County Airport and 1.25 inches in Newport.

A return southwest flow of relatively milder air caused a light accumulating snow (near an inch) to occur in New Castle County during the early morning of the 6th. The combination of the snow itself and temperatures falling to or below the freezing mark once the snow began falling caused slippery travel to occur mainly on untreated less traveled roadways as well as bridges and overpasses. The snow fell between 2 a.m. EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 6th. Precipitation started as light rain in parts of the county prior to 2 a.m. EST. Representative snowfall included 0.5 inches in Newport and 0.3 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

Snow showers, which were briefly moderate, followed a cold frontal passage and caused quick accumulations of around one inch in New Castle County on the 21st. Snow began falling after 6 p.m. EST that evening and fell moderately at times between 7 p.m. EST and 8 p.m. EST when visibilities were around one-half of a mile. The steady snow ended by 9 p.m. EST that evening. Temperatures quickly fell below freezing after the snow started and untreated roads were hazardous. Representative snowfall included 1.1 inches in Newport (New Castle County) and 1.0 inch at the New Castle County Airport, Glasgow and Newark (all in New Castle County).

A low pressure system that moved off the North Carolina coast during the evening of the 25th helped drop around one inch of snow across Delaware. The least snow fell in New Castle County while the most fell along coastal Sussex County.  The snow unfortunately coincided nearly perfectly with the Friday evening commute and resulted in numerous, but mostly minor, accidents and very long commute times. The snow began falling around 3 p.m. EST on the 25th. The snow stuck instantly as temperatures were well below freezing. The snow ended between 630 p.m. EST and 8 p.m. EST on the 25th.  Delaware State Police reported about 40 traffic accidents, the least in New Castle County. Five of the accidents resulted in injuries and there were also four disabled vehicles.||Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County), 1.5 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County) 1.4 inches in Seaford (Sussex County), 1.2 inches in Magnolia (Kent County), 1.0 inch in Viola (Kent County), Harrington (Kent County) and Ellendale (Sussex County), 0.9 inches in Delmar and Bridgeville in Sussex County, 0.8 inches in Newark (New Castle County) and Dover (Kent County) and 0.6 inches at the New Castle County Airport.|  |The low pressure system moved from the southern Rockies on the morning of the 24th into the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 25th and reached Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 25th. It then proceeded quickly offshore to the northeast. The fast movement limited the amount of snow that fell.

An area of low pressure developed across the northern plains on Thursday February 7th, tracking eastward. Meanwhile, another area of low pressure developed along the coast of North Carolina, tracking northeast. Both lows approached the area on Friday February 8th with the coastal low intensifying as it moved toward the northeast. The two lows interacted with one another with the coastal low eventually absorbing the inland low late Friday. The system moved off toward New England on Friday night, moving into the Canadian Maritimes by Saturday afternoon. As the storm intensified, winds increased and became gusty out of the northeast. Peak wind speeds included 47 mph at Ship John Shoal (Delaware Bay) and 46 mph in Lewes and Dewey Beach (Sussex County). Several locations reported 40 knots through the event. ||In addition, precipitation started to spread across the area during the morning hours on Friday February 8th. Some areas started as a mix of snow and rain before changing over to all rain during the daytime hours. A changeover to all snow occurred during the evening. Only trace amounts of snow were reported across Delaware.

A sharpening upper air trough helped develop a cold front and a weak low pressure system along it. This caused  snow showers and flurries across Delaware on the 21st. Snowfall in Kent and New Castle Counties was less than half an inch, but heavier snow (around an inch) fell in eastern Sussex County during the middle of the day on the 21st.  The snow fell at a heavy enough rate to cause accumulations on unpaved surfaces and briefly on bridges and overpasses while this was occurring in eastern Sussex County.  Representative snowfall included 1.0 inch in Lewes and Bethany Beach in Sussex County and 0.2 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

A brush fire occurred during the late afternoon of the 3rd in New Castle County near the junction of Interstates 95 and 295 close to the Newport Industrial Park and New Castle County Airport. Smoke from the fire did not impact the evening commute.  Strong west winds helped spread and hampered battling the fire. The peak wind gust at the New Castle County Airport for the day was 39 mph.

A subtle veering of the surface winds more toward the north as the week's nor'easter only slowly drifted offshore brought areas of moderate tidal flooding back to Sussex County with the high tide cycle on the morning of the 9th.  The high tide at Breakwater Harbor reached 6.85 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water.  Minor tidal flooding continued with the next two high tide cycles and even extended up Delaware Bay and the tidal Delaware River through Reedy Island (7.39 feet above mean lower low water the highest tide).

A subtle veering of the surface winds more toward the north as the week's nor'easter only slowly drifted offshore brought areas of moderate tidal flooding back to Sussex County with the high tide cycle on the morning of the 9th.  The high tide at Breakwater Harbor reached 6.85 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water.  Minor tidal flooding continued with the next two high tide cycles and even extended up Delaware Bay and the tidal Delaware River through Reedy Island (7.39 feet above mean lower low water the highest tide).

An intense nor'easter brought strong to high northeast winds across Delaware on the 6th as well as minor to moderate tidal flooding in central and lower Delaware Bay.  The coastal flooding caused a new breach along Delaware State Route 1 and flooded other roadways. The coastal flooding was exacerbated by wave action as waves at Bethany Beach reached 12 feet. Voluntary evacuations were encouraged for flood prone areas in Kent and Sussex Counties. Peak wind gusts reached around 70 mph and downed trees, tree limbs and wires and caused some structural damage. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power, hardest hit were Kent and Sussex Counties. Some schools throughout the state were closed on the 6th. At least minor tidal flooding persisted into the morning high tide cycle on the 10th.  This was the greatest and most persistent tidal flooding to affect the Delaware coast since Hurricane Sandy last October. ||Minor tidal flooding started with the high tide cycle on the morning of the 6th. Moderate tidal flooding occurred with both the afternoon high tide cycle on the 6th and the morning high tide cycle on the 7th. Minor tidal flooding also occurred with both high tide cycles on the 8th. The highest tide at Breakwater Harbor reached 7.38 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water. ||The coastal flooding closed approximately 15 roads in Kent and Sussex Counties.  In Sussex County, waves breached a dune north of the Indian River Inlet Bridge and forced the closure of Delaware State Route 1 between Dewey Beach and Bethany Beach as well as the Indian River Inlet Bridge. The roadway remained closed through the 7th. In Bethany Beach, roadways north of Delaware State Route 26 were closed. Northeast of Milton, Prime Hook Road was closed and remained closed through March 13th.  In Lewes (Sussex County), the Canal Bridge, Canry Creek Road and New Road were all flooded. Flooded roadways were also reported in Broadkill Beach, Angola and Oak Orchard (River and Oak Orchard Roads).  In Kent County, roadways were flooded and closed at Big Stone Beach,  Bennett's Park, Pickering Beach, Kitt Hummock, Woodland Beach, South Bowers Beach, Bowers Beach and  Port Mahon Road. Inland flooding affected Delaware State Route 9, Cypress Branch Road and Magnolia Road south of Dover. ||Northeast winds intensified as the morning of the 6th continued and reached their peak during the afternoon. As winds slowly backed to the north late that afternoon, wind speeds diminished. The high winds tore shingles from homes in Bear (New Castle County). High winds also damaged a home in Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County). The high winds littered  numerous Kent County roadways with tree debris. The high winds forced the Cape May-Lewes Ferry to cancel four Delaware Bay crossings on the 6th. Normal ferry service resumed on the 7th. ||Peak wind gusts included 71 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 66 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 64 mph in Odessa (New Castle County), 61 mph in Georgetown (Sussex County), 58 mph in Milton (Sussex County), 55 mph in Bear (New Castle County), 54 mph in Harrington (Kent County), 49 mph in Dover (Kent County) and 48 mph at the New Castle County Airport. ||The nor'easter low pressure system emerged from the southern Rockies on the 4th and moved into the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 5th, passed across the southern Appalachians during the evening of the 5th and reached northeastern North Carolina on the morning of the 6th. From there it slowly moved northeast and was off the Delmarva Peninsula on the afternoon of the 6th. It then drifted slowly offshore to the east that evening and that motion continued on the 7th and 8th. The low pressure system was not that intense overall (never deepened to less than 985 millibars near the coast), but a strong high pressure system that was located over southeastern Canada helped intensify the surface pressure gradient (difference) throughout this event.

It then drifted slowly offshore to the east that evening and that motion continued on the 7th and 8th. The low pressure system was not that intense overall (never deepened to less than 985 millibars near the coast), but a strong high pressure system that was located over southeastern Canada helped intensify the surface pressure gradient (difference) throughout this event.

A nor'easter that moved off the Delaware coast on the 25th dropped 1 to 4 inches of snow across the state with the highest snowfall in New Castle County.  Most of the snow fell during the day and had difficulty accumulating when the intensity decreased later in the morning through the afternoon and early evening. Nevertheless, the heaviest snow fell during the morning commute and caused slushy roadways and numerous traffic accidents.  Delaware State Police responded to nearly 100 traffic accidents, the most in Kent County. There were also nearly 20 disabled vehicles and 5 accidents resulting in injuries in Sussex County. A school bus crashed (without injuries) near U.S. Route 13 in Seaford. Dozens of schools were closed for the day or announced plans to close early and cancel afternoon and evening activities. The precipitation started as rain (briefly in New Castle County) before sunrise on the 25th and changed over to snow from north to south between 5 a.m. and 8 a.m. EDT. The snow fell at its heaviest during the rest of the morning. While snow fell during the afternoon, it did not accumulate efficiently. As precipitation intensity lessened, the snow changed to light rain during the late afternoon and early evening of the 25th and the rain ended later that evening.||Representative snowfall included 4.3 inches in Glasglow (New Castle County), 3.3 inches in Hockessin (New Castle County), 3.1 inches in Greenville (New Castle County), 2.8 inches in Viola (Kent County) and Odessa (New Castle County), 2.5 inches in Magnolia (Kent County), 2.4 inches in Newark (New Castle County), 2.3 inches in Dover (Kent County),  1.5 inches in Bridgeville (Sussex County), 1.3 inches in Ellendale (Sussex County), 1.1 inches at the New Castle County Airport and 0.8 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County).||The low pressure system responsible for the late season snow moved from Alabama on the morning of the 24th northeast and passed just east of Norfolk, Virginia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 25th. It proceeded to move northeast and by 8 p.m. EDT on the 25th it was about 175 miles east of the New Jersey coast.

The pressure gradient (difference)  between a high pressure system over the Gulf Coast States and a low pressure system over Eastern Canada produced strong gusty west to northwest winds in New Castle County Delaware during the day on the 14th. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and knocked down weak tree limbs and wires. It was not as windy in lower Delaware. Peak wind gusts included 51 mph in Odessa and 44 mph at the New Castle County Airport.

A sharpening upper air trough helped develop a cold front and a weak low pressure system along it. This caused  snow showers and flurries across Delaware on the 21st. Snowfall in Kent and New Castle Counties was less than half an inch, but heavier snow (around an inch) fell in eastern Sussex County during the middle of the day on the 21st.  The snow fell at a heavy enough rate to cause accumulations on unpaved surfaces and briefly on bridges and overpasses while this was occurring in eastern Sussex County.  Representative snowfall included 1.0 inch in Lewes and Bethany Beach in Sussex County and 0.2 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

The combination of a strong northwest flow around an anomalously strong (for late May) low pressure system slowly moving through the New England coastal waters and larger tidal spreads associated with the full moon caused pockets of blowout tides to occur during both low tide cycles on the 25th along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries. The lowest tide at Reedy Island reached 1.69 feet  below mean lower low water.  The lowest tide at Delaware City reached 1.70 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

Strong northwest winds circulating around a slow moving low pressure system traveling through the New England coastal waters affected Delaware during the daytime on the 25th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and damaged some weak tree limbs and wires. Peak wind gusts included 47 mph in Newark (New Castle County) and Lewes (Sussex County) and 44 mph in Saint Georges (New Castle County).

A strong northwest flow following a cold frontal passage produced wind gusts as high as around 45 mph inland and around 50 mph at the coast across Delaware from the evening of the 23rd into the afternoon of the 24th. The strong winds came in two spurts, one just following the cold frontal passage on the 23rd and a more prolonged and sustained period from the pre-dawn hours into the middle of the afternoon on the 24th. Winds started diminishing as a strong high pressure system came closer to the area. Peak wind gusts included 52 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 51 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 46 mph in Smyrna (New Castle County), Harrington (Kent County) and Ellendale (Sussex County) and 44 mph at the New Castle County Airport.

The strong winds came in two spurts, one just following the cold frontal passage on the 23rd and a more prolonged and sustained period from the pre-dawn hours into the middle of the afternoon on the 24th. Winds started diminishing as a strong high pressure system came closer to the area. Peak wind gusts included 52 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 51 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 46 mph in Smyrna (New Castle County), Harrington (Kent County) and Ellendale (Sussex County) and 44 mph at the New Castle County Airport.

A strong cold front produced a convective line of showers and scattered thunderstorms with damaging winds that moved through the northern half of Delaware before weakening during the morning of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Greenville in Christina Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced a convective line of showers and isolated thunderstorms produced widespread wind gusts of around 40 mph as it moved through Delaware.  Isolated damaging winds occurred in New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A convective line knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Newark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that formed over the southeastern United States on the 17th brought rain and then snow to Sussex County. Precipitation started as rain on the 17th, but then changed to snow during the evening of the 17th and ended early in the morning on the 18th.  Accumulations ranged from around half an inch to two inches in the southeast half of the county.  Untreated smaller roadways as well as bridges and overpasses were hazardous.  Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Bethany, 1.0 inch in Selbyville and 0.5 inches in Lewes, Seaford and Delmar.  The low pressure moved from near Atlanta, Georgia on the morning of the 17th slowly eastnortheast and passed into the Atlantic Ocean near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 17th. From there it accelerated northeast and by 7 a.m. EST on the 18th, it was well southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.  The initial lack of cold air and the southern storm track both limited the northward extension of the precipitation and had most of the precipitation fall as rain until the end.

Light snow that fell during the early morning of the 24th resulted in snow covered roads and accidents, mainly in Sussex County. Accumulations were 1 to 2 inches in Sussex County and less than half an inch in Kent and New Castle Counties. Snow began falling shortly after midnight EST on the 24th and ended around 7 a.m. EST that morning. Temperatures were below freezing and the snow stuck instantly to untreated roadways.  Sussex County Police reported about 20 traffic accidents, three with injuries and two disabled vehicles. Representative snowfall included 1.6 inches in Selbyville (Sussex County), 1.0 inch in Georgetown, Delmar and Seaford (all Sussex County) and 0.4 inches in Dover (Kent County). The light snow was caused by a low pressure system that moved west to east from Missouri on the afternoon of the 23rd and passed off the North Carolina coast around 6 a.m. EST on the 24th.

A complex low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes during the 8th and the 9th, allowing an associated frontal boundary to move over the northern Mid Atlantic Region on the 8th, with a secondary area of low pressure forming off the Mid Atlantic coast.  Significant forcing and lift within the atmosphere resulted in an intense band of snow developing across northern Maryland during the morning of the 8th.  This heavy snow band pushed east, moving over portions of northern Maryland, northern Delaware, southeastern Pennsylvania, and into central to southern New Jersey during the afternoon.  Heavy snow fell for several hours, and areas under this banding feature received in excess of 6 inches of snow, with the highest snowfall amounts reaching 12 inches.  Snowfall rates within this intense snow band reached 2 to 3 inches per hour for a time.  Outside of this snow band and its effected area, most locations received between 1 and 4 inches of snow, with mainly less than an inch falling across northern New Jersey and southern parts of the Delmarva. ||By late afternoon to the evening of the 8th, the stronger forcing and banding of precipitation diminished, but with warmer air moving into the region, a transition to sleet, freezing rain, and rain occurred from southeast to northwest into the night of the 8th and the early morning of the 9th.  The transition to rain occurred more quickly across the Delmarva and into portions of southern New Jersey, while cold air lingering at the surface resulted in freezing rain lasting into the night from around the Lehigh Valley, the Poconos, and into northwest New Jersey.  Ice accretion was reported across the area, with ice accumulations in the one tenth to one quarter inch range, mainly from the Interstate 95 corridor on north and westward.||Representative snowfall totals included 12.0 inches in Newark (New Castle County), 10.3 inches four miles southwest of Newark (New Castle County), 8.5 inches at the New Castle County Airport In Wilmington (New Castle County), 7.1 inches in Newport (New Castle County), 6.4 inches in Claymont (New Castle County), 5.9 inches in Greenville (New Castle County), 3.5 inches in Odessa (New Castle County), 2.3 inches in Townsend (New Castle County), 0.9 inches in Dover (Kent County), 0.6 inches in Viola (Kent County), 0.4 inches in Harrington (Kent County) and in Laurel (Sussex County) and also in Selbyville (Sussex County), and 0.3 inches of snow in Seaford (Sussex County).  Representative ice accumulations included 0.12 inch ice accretion in New Castle (New Castle County).||Heavy snow, along with some sleet and freezing rain, resulted in hazardous travel across Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 8th, most notably in New Castle County.  The heavy snow forced the closure of the Delaware Memorial Bridge.  Many roads in New Castle County were impassable for a time due to multiple vehicles being stuck, and some main roads were down to only one or two lanes.  A jack-knife tractor trailer blocked the intersection of Route 7 northbound at Churchman's Road.  In addition, Papermill Road was impassable due to at least forty vehicles being stuck in the snow.

The very persistent and strong northwest flow that started during the evening of the 23rd caused blowout tide conditions during the morning low tide cycle on the 24th along tidal sections of the Delaware River. At Reedy Island, the low tide reached 1.90 feet below mean lower low water.  Blowout tides start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

Light snow that fell during the early morning of the 24th resulted in snow covered roads and accidents, mainly in Sussex County. Accumulations were 1 to 2 inches in Sussex County and less than half an inch in Kent and New Castle Counties. Snow began falling shortly after midnight EST on the 24th and ended around 7 a.m. EST that morning. Temperatures were below freezing and the snow stuck instantly to untreated roadways.  Sussex County Police reported about 20 traffic accidents, three with injuries and two disabled vehicles. Representative snowfall included 1.6 inches in Selbyville (Sussex County), 1.0 inch in Georgetown, Delmar and Seaford (all Sussex County) and 0.4 inches in Dover (Kent County). The light snow was caused by a low pressure system that moved west to east from Missouri on the afternoon of the 23rd and passed off the North Carolina coast around 6 a.m. EST on the 24th.

An unseasonably hot and humid air mass caused high temperatures to reach into the lower 90s on the 11th. Combined with high dew point levels, afternoon heat index values reached around 100F throughout the state.  Maximum temperatures included 93 degrees in Georgetown (Sussex County) and Dover (Kent County) and 91 degrees at the New Castle County Airport.  The core of the hot air mass moved offshore at night, so high temperatures (and heat index values) were slightly lower on the 12th. A cold frontal passage the night of the 12th brought the end of 90 degree days for 2013.

A persistent onshore flow caused periods of heavy rain, strong winds, beach erosion and widespread minor tidal flooding to occur across Delaware from the 9th through the 13th.  Strong winds and minor tidal flooding started with the daytime high tide cycle on the 9th. The winds and minor tidal flooding both peaked on the 10th, with very slow improvement in conditions through Sunday the 13th on Columbus Day weekend and not so coincidentally the last day that minor tidal flooding occurred.||In Sussex County, minor tidal flooding affected roadways in the Long Neck area. Roadway tidal flooding occurred in and around Angola and Oak Orchard. Water was over some bulkheads. In Milton, minor tidal flooding closed Prime Hook Road through the Back Bay Cove. The roadway remained closed for a couple of days. It was reopened after debris was removed from it. The Delaware Department of Transportation provided residents of Prime Hook Road access to their homes. The high tides reached the support pilings of homes at Primehook Beach.  Broadkill Road (Delaware State Route 16) was also flooded. ||In Kent County, minor tidal flooding affected Bowers Beach. A dune breach occurred. A parking lot off of Murderkill Avenue was flooded. Scattered street flooding and street closings occurred along Delaware Bay between Dover and Milford. This included Port Mahon Road. The Delaware State University cancelled some outdoor activities scheduled for the 11th and 12th. In New Castle County, The strong winds caused wave action to lap over a sea wall in Bay View Beach south of Port Penn.   ||While minor tidal flooding occurred during the daytime high tide cycles from the 9th through the 13th in Kent and Sussex Counties, the highest tides occurred during the daytime high tide cycle on the 10th. The high tide at Lewes (Breakwater Harbor) reached 6.77 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water. This also coincided with the strongest winds to affect the area as most of the peak wind gusts occurred during the first half of the day on the 10th and included 50 mph at Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 49 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and 39 mph in Indian River (Sussex County).  The strong winds blew sand into the left lane of northbound Delaware State Route 1 near the Indian River Inlet Bridge.||The tidal flooding and strong winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a high pressure system over New England and a low pressure system along the Atlantic seaboard. This low initially formed on a cold front in the Gulf of Mexico on the 7th. Some of the moisture associated with Tropical Storm Karen was ingested into this system. This low pressure system moved to the Georgia coast on the morning of the 8th and the North Carolina coast on the morning of the 9th. This low pressure system remained near the Outer Banks of North Carolina into the morning of the 10th (when the pressure difference between it and the high pressure system over New England peaked) before slowly weakening and drifting north to the Delmarva coastal waters on the 11th and 12th. A new high pressure system building into New England on the 13th then started shoving the weakening low pressure system southward later in the day on the 13th and into the 14th.
