Heavy rain associated with the nor'easter that stalled off the Delmarva Peninsula and North Carolina coast caused poor drainage flooding as well as some minor flooding along the Red Clay Creek Basin on the 11th. Rain began falling overnight on the 9th, continued during the day on the 10th, and fell at its heaviest during the first half of the day on the 11th. The rain ended overnight on the 11th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches in New Castle County, with some higher amounts in the headwaters of the Red Clay Creek in Chester County, Pennsylvania. Event precipitation totals over four days reached 3.0 to 6.0 inches in Kent and Sussex Counties, but any reported flooding was limited to fields and poor drainage locations. Dry antecedent conditions prevented flooding from being worse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain that fell early on the 11th caused minor flooding along the Red Clay Creek Basin on the morning and afternoon of the 11th. The heavy rain also caused poor drainage flooding in and around Newark and Middletown.  The Red Clay Creek at Wooddale was above its 7 foot flood stage from 832 a.m. EDT through 1023 a.m. EDT on the 11th. It crested at 7.15 feet at 915 a.m. EDT on the 11th. Farther downstream, the Red Clay Creek at Stanton was above its 15 foot flood stage from 916 a.m. EDT through 1239 p.m. EDT on the 11th. It crested at 15.30 feet at 1030 a.m. EDT on the 11th. Event precipitation totals included 2.90 inches in Blackbird, 1.98 inches in Newark and 1.50 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud in southern Delaware during the afternoon of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud that was seen by several eyewitnesses over a corn field near Railway Road and Delaware State Route 26 in Millville.  While eyewitness accounts stated that the funnel came close to the ground, no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a lee side trof and an approaching cold front triggered scattered thunderstorms mainly across northern Delaware during the daytime and evening of the 2nd. An isolated severe thunderstorm occurred in New Castle County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced damaging wind gusts in western New Castle County along with some small hail (around half an inch in diameter). In Hockessin, large tree limbs were knocked down along Brackenville Road. In Lindell, several large trees were knocked down along the Mill Creek from Limestone Road south to Kirkwood Highway. While the thunderstorm weakened as it moved into eastern New Castle County, it still produced some pea size hail and a wind gust to 45 mph at the New Castle County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused flash flooding in the northern half of Delaware. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in northeastern New Castle County and around 6 inches along the New Castle County and Kent County border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused small stream, poor drainage and field flash flooding along the northern tier of Kent County affecting Duck Creek and Kenton Hundreds and Smyrna and Clayton the hardest. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 5 inches in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused flash flooding in the northern half of Delaware. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in northeastern New Castle County and around 6 inches along the New Castle County and Kent County border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused small stream, poor drainage and field flash flooding along the southern northern tier of New Castle County affecting Blackbird and Appoquinimink Hundreds the hardest.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 6 inches in this area. Actual rainfall amounts included 3.34 inches in Blackbird Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front which not only produced flash flooding but also produced isolated wind damage in New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Wilmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy downpours in Sussex County Delaware during the evening of the 9th. Doppler Radar and storm total measurements reached around 2.5 inches in the central part of the county, most of which fell within one hour and caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours, over 2 inches fell within an hour, caused flash flooding of smaller creeks as well as field and poor drainage flooding in and to the south of Georgetown in central Sussex County. Event precipitation totals included 2.53 inches in Georgetown and 2.48 inches in Stockley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of thunderstorms with very heavy downpours in Delaware mainly during the late morning and afternoon of the 1st. Event totals were 1 to 4 inches throughout most of the state. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as some flash flooding in Sussex County and minor flooding along the Christina River in New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding of small streams, fields as well as poor drainage flooding in southeastern Sussex County. Event precipitation totals included 4.13 inches in Bethany Beach, 3.80 inches in Selbyville and 3.50 inches in Gumboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of thunderstorms with very heavy downpours in Delaware mainly during the late morning and afternoon of the 1st. Event totals were 1 to 4 inches throughout most of the state. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as some flash flooding in Sussex County and minor flooding along the Christina River in New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding of small streams and fields as well as poor drainage flooding in and around Ellendale in central Sussex County. Event precipitation totals included 4.24 inches in Ellendale and 2.82 inches in Redden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of thunderstorms with very heavy downpours in Delaware mainly during the late morning and afternoon of the 1st. Event totals were 1 to 4 inches throughout most of the state. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as some flash flooding in Sussex County and minor flooding along the Christina River in New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain and wet antecedent conditions caused minor flooding along the Christina River. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 1204 p.m. EDT through 303 p.m. EDT on the 1st. It crested at 11.34 feet at 115 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.90 inches in Newport, 1.31 inches in Newark, 1.17 inches at the New Castle County Airport and 1.03 inches in Hockessin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex of showers and strong to severe thunderstorms trailing an advancing warm front caused wind damage and flash flooding in New Castle County during the morning of the 13th. The combination of flooding and damaging winds caused more than 15,000 Delmarva Power and Light customers to lose power in northern Delaware and Maryland. About 1,200 customers were still without power that afternoon.  The thunderstorms also caused twenty to forty minute delays in Amtrak Service between Wilmington and Baltimore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved through Newark sheared the bricks off of one building and also damaged shutters and windows at the Studio Green Apartments. Trees and power lines were also knocked down along Elkton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex of showers and strong to severe thunderstorms trailing an advancing warm front caused wind damage and flash flooding in New Castle County during the morning of the 13th. The combination of flooding and damaging winds caused more than 15,000 Delmarva Power and Light customers to lose power in northern Delaware and Maryland. About 1,200 customers were still without power that afternoon.  The thunderstorms also caused twenty to forty minute delays in Amtrak Service between Wilmington and Baltimore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Bear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex of showers and strong to severe thunderstorms trailing an advancing warm front caused wind damage and flash flooding in New Castle County during the morning of the 13th. The combination of flooding and damaging winds caused more than 15,000 Delmarva Power and Light customers to lose power in northern Delaware and Maryland. About 1,200 customers were still without power that afternoon.  The thunderstorms also caused twenty to forty minute delays in Amtrak Service between Wilmington and Baltimore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees along U.S. Route 40 in Glasgow. The westbound lanes of the roadway were closed at LaGrange Avenue and large trees were knocked down in Glasgow Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding in New Castle County as it moved through during the morning of the 13th. Event storm totals averaged 3 to 4 inches.  In addition to widespread poor drainage flooding, flooding also affected the Christina River and the White Clay Creek into the afternoon of the 13th. Because the heaviest rain and flooded roadways coincided with the morning commute, travel times quadrupled for some motorists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused, creek, river as well as urban and poor drainage flash flooding in the northern half of New Castle County that started during the morning of the 13th. Hardest hit were Newark and New Castle. Delaware State Route 7 was covered with several feet of water in Pike Creek (Mill Creek Hundred) and also flooded in Newark. Flooding was also reported along South Chapel Street and Lovett Avenue in Newark, along Harmony Road east of Newark as well as on Delaware State Routes 72 and 896.  In New Castle, firefighters rescued stranded motorists on U.S. Route 13. Delaware State Route 273 and Ferry Cutoff Road were also flooded. U.S. Route 13 flooded in a couple of other locations including within Wilmington. Delaware State Route 141 flooded in Newport. The Nonsuch Creek flooded Airport Road near the New Castle County Airport. Flooding also occurred along the Christina River and White Clay Creek. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge flooded on the 13th, but the gage failed to report during the entire event. The river went below flood stage at 259 p.m. EDT on the 13th. The highest reported reading (at moderate flooding levels) was 12.15 feet at 130 p.m. EDT, but the actual crest may have been higher. The White Clay Creek east of Newark was above its 13 foot flood stage from 812 a.m. EDT through 1055 a.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 14.82 feet at 915 a.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 3.29 inches in Wilmington, 3.26 inches in Newport, 3.12 inches at Newark and 3.10 inches at the New Castle County Airport.
